does: This paper proposes Single Image Unlearning (SIU), a method to remove visual recognition of specific concepts from multimodal large language models by fine-tuning on a single image for few steps. The approach constructs multifaceted fine-tuning data using four distinct targets representing different aspects of the concept to forget, then jointly trains the model using a novel Dual Masked KL-divergence Loss combined with Cross Entropy loss. This dual objective simultaneously forgets the target concept's visual features while preserving the MLLM's general utility and performance on other tasks. The authors establish MMUBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning in multimodal models, and introduce metrics specifically designed for assessing both forgetting effectiveness and model utility retention.
connects: This work extends machine unlearning research from traditional machine learning and single-modality models to the emerging domain of multimodal large language models. It builds on prior machine unlearning literature that addresses the right to be forgotten, but adapts these approaches to handle the unique challenge of visual concept forgetting in vision-language systems. The method responds to concerns about data privacy and concept leakage in increasingly powerful multimodal models, connecting to broader debates about responsible AI and data protection in foundation models. The use of KL-divergence-based objectives relates to existing work in model editing and controlled forgetting, while the focus on visual information removal specifically addresses gaps in applying unlearning techniques to the visual components of multimodal systems.
enables: This work opens directions for efficient privacy-preserving machine learning in multimodal systems, enabling practical implementation of data removal rights in large deployed models without full retraining. The established MMUBench benchmark provides infrastructure for future research on unlearning in multimodal contexts, facilitating comparison of competing approaches. The single-image fine-tuning efficiency suggests potential applications to personalized model updates and concept removal at scale. Future work could extend the method to remove multiple concepts simultaneously, improve robustness against adversarial attempts to recover forgotten information, and apply similar techniques to other multimodal architectures and modalities beyond vision-language models, advancing the broader goal of responsible AI systems that respect individual privacy.
